items.myth: Recursion is always slower than iteration.
items.reality: Per call there's stack-frame overhead, but for tree-shaped problems the recursive form is often simpler AND has the same Big-O. With memoization it can be dramatically faster than naïve iteration.

items.myth: Python optimizes tail calls into loops.
items.reality: It doesn't. Tail-recursive code in Python still grows the stack on each call. Guido decided against TCO in CPython on purpose (tracebacks would lose the call chain).
items.example:
```python
def tail(n, acc=0):
    if n == 0: return acc
    return tail(n - 1, acc + n)   # still grows the stack in CPython
```

items.reality: It raises a soft limit but each frame still costs C-stack memory. A genuinely deep recursion can crash the interpreter outright. Convert to iteration for any 'unbounded depth' input.
items.example:
```python
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(100_000)   # quick fix; still risky
```

items.myth: Recursion and dynamic programming are different things.
items.reality: Top-down DP IS memoized recursion. Bottom-up DP is the iterative form of the same recurrence. Both compute identical things; pick by which is easier to express for the recurrence at hand.
items.example:
```python
# Top-down: @lru_cache + recursive recurrence
# Bottom-up: fill a table from smallest sub-problem upward
```

items.reality: Tail recursion means the recursive call is the *last* operation in the function (no work to do after it returns). It is unrelated to whether recursion terminates.
items.example:
```python
def tail(n, acc=0):
    if n == 0: return acc
    return tail(n - 1, acc + n)        # tail call: no work after

def not_tail(n):
    if n == 0: return 0
    return 1 + not_tail(n - 1)         # not tail: still adds 1 after return
```
